speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 6, 1995. Survivor-- Matilda Klein Weisel Interviewer-- Naomi Rappaport. City-- Rego Park. State-- New York. Country-- USA. Language-- English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Naomi Rappaport, and today is September 6, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Matilda Weisel Klein in Rego Park, New York, USA. Would you please give us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Matilda Klein Weisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. M-A-T-I-L-D-A K-L-E-I-N. And Weisel is W-I-E-S-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On the birth certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a Yiddish name. And what was that, please? Yenta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Y-E-N-T-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The birth date is March 18, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Swalava.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-W-A-L-A-V-A

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much, Mrs. Klein, for letting us come into your home and telling us your story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd like to start by asking you a little bit about your family-- your parents, your grandparents. What were your parents' names? And where do they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were born in the same city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: Swalava.

speaker: interviewer
text: Swalava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. And we lived there till 1945, when they took us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: '44. What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fanny Bluma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe Yosef.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your grandparents live in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Swalava as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large is Swalava? How large a community is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know how many Jews lived there-- around 300 families. But it was a city with 10,000 population. So it wasn't a town. It was a little bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was bigger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where exactly in Czechoslovakia was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Carpathian mountains. And not far from Munkacs. Munkacs was the big city. But the capital city was Ungwar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ungwar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how far where are you from Ungwar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 50 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Jewish population do there? What kind of economy were they involved with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in business. They had stores. And they were tailors and shoe makers, watch makers. We had one doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do? Did your father-- did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a government employee. He worked in an office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a foreman in a factory, where they had-- oh, most of the population lived from the forests-- wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And we had big factories where they produced wood and sent it to the whole world. And they were building ships out of this wood. So in this wood factory, my-- my father was a foreman and worked in the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was government-owned?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a government-owned, yeah. And he had very good benefits and a good pension plan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father-- was he educated in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a general, secular education, or a religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had two grades. He went to second grade to public school, and the rest in a Yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Were there many Jews working for the Czech government? Would you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. They did, yeah. But the non-Jewish population, almost all of them worked in this big factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live? Do you remember your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember my house. It was close to a river, and we had beautiful gardens. And the outside, outdoors-- we spend our whole time outdoors, running around, playing around. It-- it was very fresh air in the mountains. It looks like Switzerland. But the people were poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered poor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say you remember your house, how large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two houses. And we lived in one house, and the other was rented. We had tenants who paid rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you owned your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many rooms were in the house in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three bedrooms and a big kitchen. Yeah. We were a big family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived in which rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept with the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. You slept-- how many boys were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two boys and me. And one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other three?

speaker: interviewee
text: In another room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents had their own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were meals eaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about on holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a special room-- one of the bedrooms. But the bedroom had couches. And the holidays were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Who were your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousins. We were very close with the cousins. We-- we grew up together. It was a big family with a lot of kids, and we were always together. We went to school together. We played around. And we spent the evenings together with the cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any non-Jews living near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship between you and the-- who were the non-Jews, Czechs or Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They-- how did they call them? They were like Russians, Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they were not educated. And they went to work into this factory. And when they get their salary, they went straight to the bar and drink it away, the two weeks' salary. And they didn't have money to pay the rent. And we kept 10 tenants like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had 10 tenants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But every family lived in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you had 10 families right next door to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever play with their children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like playing with them? Were they friendly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were friendly, and some of them were bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any relationship with the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did. She did. Yes, she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your house, or you came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to our house, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever go to their house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family was observant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Hasidic? Did they wear a Gartel and a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did your parents wear, your father wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. My father passed away when I was two years old, so I can't tell you--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what he was wearing. But he had a beard. I have a picture of him. And the mother was wearing black.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the-- was she wearing black because she was in mourning?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in mourning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other women-- your aunts, your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also black.

speaker: interviewer
text: They also wore black?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never wore color of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a little flowery thing here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children? What did you wear? Do you remember your clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- long sleeve, stockings, and the hair was long with braids. And on shabbat, I was not supposed to comb my hair. And we had-- we had flowery dresses, yes. The children were lively, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody wear Hasidic garb, shtreimel and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. They did, but not in my family. Just in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech school, public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: With other Jewish children, or with non-Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship between the children and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not good. Do you remember any incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The non-Jewish were beating us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the teachers do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teacher was anti-Semitic. The teacher was beating, to, for every little thing. The teacher had a stick, and smack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the kids-- because I'm a Jew. From the teacher and from the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way that anybody could protest, could protect themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother took me to the police once when I was beaten up by a teacher. And the police send me to a doctor. If he has a doctor's certificate, then they can do nothing. If not, they can't do nothing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor wouldn't give the certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother healed my wounds, and it went away, till I get beaten up again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just you, or lots of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just me-- lots of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lots of children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet you continued to play with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was avoiding them. The ones who were-- who were beating, I was avoiding them. And some of them who were not beating, we had a relationship, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- how many years did you spend in this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language were you taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech. And at home, what language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did kids do to protect themselves? Were there any clubs or any organizations that-- where they could defend themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They were hiding. We were going home from school. It was a long walk. So we were going in groups and not when the rest of the kids go. We let them go home so they wouldn't beat us up separately, later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any situation of a relationship where people were socially friendly with the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any organized anti-Semitism in your town when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have to be organized. They were brought up this way. They didn't need an organization for this. They were brought up this way, to hate the Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back to some of the things that happened in your life as you were growing up. Holidays-- how were they celebrated in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the most beautiful things, were the holidays. My memories go back. The nicest things were the holidays and the Friday nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made Friday nights, since your father was not alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brothers. How much older are your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: How much older? Two years apart. We are all two years apart-- every two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were old enough to make shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. They made beautiful shabbat with Zemirot, and the good food was also good, with white tablecloths and with candles and singing and telling jokes. This was the most beautiful things I remember back from my childhood, the good part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she have anyone to help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted me to help her, but I didn't like it. But I helped anyway. I had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the oldest girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an older sister, but she passed away. So then I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What holidays were the most exciting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were wearing all kind of costumes and going from house to house. Simchat Torah was a beautiful holiday. And every Friday night was a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till this day. I made the same holidays out of Friday nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any special treatment because you were an orphan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In school. I got free books. The rest of the kids had to buy their books, and I got them from the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Kehillah? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had poor people to take care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had poor people to take care of, the Kehillah. We weren't that poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Primarily because your father had a pension?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We had a steady income for a while. But when the Hungarians took over, the income stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on-- from what did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: From tourists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seriously, what-- how-- how were you able to manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we were able to-- we had a garden, which we had our own vegetable and fruit. And we had our own milk and milk products. So we didn't have to shop much. So mother was trying to do a little black market business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buying and selling some stuff, and we helped her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any kind of clubs? What did young kids do for social life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to Hebrew school. But it wasn't called Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Cheder was for boys. The girls, they didn't call it a Cheder. And we learned German and some other stuff. We had a teacher, but this was just once a week. We had this [INAUDIBLE]. And then on shabbat, we made exercise and songs and dances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Zionist group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Just a Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And who taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ladies teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lady teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get their education? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were local people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Local. And they were just for girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you belong to any kind of groups-- Zionist groups or Bundist groups or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was against Zionism. The boys belonged.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: A [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was what kind of club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not Zionist. We were-- Mother was against it. And they met, and they were-- they made plays for Hanukkah-- plays. And they had some activity. I don't remember what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get newspapers in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which papers did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish paper, but I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland. And some books, too, that came from Poland. I don't remember what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you read books?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, but not Yiddish books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which kind of books did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read Czech and Hungarian books, and I borrow it from a teacher. We didn't have a library.

speaker: interviewer
text: The community did not have a library?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you read these books, were these translations, or were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Translations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what kind of books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Translations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what kind of books?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Pearl Buck.

speaker: interviewer
text: You read Pearl Buck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. When I was 12 years old. It was a translation. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How aware were you of what was going on in Poland and Germany, Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about it because one of my brothers was listening to underground radio BBC from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the division of Czechoslovakia, or before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. After.

speaker: interviewer
text: After.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So every day, we knew the news, what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of Nazism and concentration camps at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't.

speaker: interviewee
text: They never said it on the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things change when Czechoslovakia was divided and your community became Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: A tremendous change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How? How did it change?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the Hungarian took away all the businesses from the Jews. They took away their houses. And they took away the men for work-- work labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that-- Did that have a name, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What? [INAUDIBLE] --

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Work labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this part of the Hungarian labor force?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young men. The young men from 20 up to 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- where-- were they allowed to remain in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear from them? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come home at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. Very seldom. But we were sending them packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime they did, sometime they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was in the Ukraine, and one was in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were writing letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Hungarians, yes-- one or two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What else changed? We just have to fight for survival. If my mother walked with me on the sidewalk and then a Hungarian soldier came across, so he pushed her down into the mud because this is his way to walk. And we couldn't go out in the evening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It got dark, we had to be locked up in the house. We were afraid to go out. We weren't allowed to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: A curfew, right-- curfew. And some of the Jews-- not all of them-- had to go every month to the police and to sign themselves in. And they were many times beaten up by the police. And if we saw a policeman, we were scared to death. And if we had a little food in the house, we were hiding it because we were afraid of search.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remained in your own house, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't take your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- The Hungarians took over, but yours was a Czechoslovakian community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the Czech people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all left.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It wasn't many. It wasn't many.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, you had Hungarians and Ukrainians there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else changed under the Hungarian rule?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was many young woman who didn't have the husbands with little kids. And they were miserable. They tried to make a living somehow. All of them became a seamstress and all kind of work so they could maintain to feed the children. It was a big change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- did the Kehillah take over anything then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did, yes. The Kehillah-- they had-- every day, they give poor people milk and bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the Kehillah? Who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sisterhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sisterhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who determined who should get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever came. Whoever came for food, for milk and bread, they got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone there who had money, who was rich or had power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. There were a few rich people. Yeah. They were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody make any attempt to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where to? No. There was no place to go. We were just hoping God will help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the rabbis tell you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. They prayed. They didn't tell what to do, because they didn't have a solution for us. So what can they tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left in your house when your brothers went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where to?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Hungarian labor force.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother and one of my brothers, me, and a little sister-- three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Try to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no-- I didn't have to work. But I was sent to Munkacs, to the big city, and bought some food. And I smuggled it in with the students, because in the-- students were going to high school, into the big city. And I was traveling with the students. And the students weren't searched. So I smuggled in some food, and we were selling it for the neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the students Jewish or non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. Both. Yeah. And we tried to sell it to our tenants. And that's how we made a little profit.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first come in contact with Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did they enter your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian enter my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938. Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Army. And what happened in 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans didn't came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these the same Hungarians that were living there, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were they different Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They came from the mainland. And they were the scum of the earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who didn't like to work, who didn't have a home. They became soldiers, and they were the captors. They send them to our little country to keep on-- be the bad guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They beat-- beat up people. And they took away the-- the-- all the livelihood from the Jews. That's what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal experiences with the Hungarian army, or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --people who were running the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was protected by Mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences with these soldiers in taking away your things, your-- not just you, your whole household?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never took your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point were new laws set up? Did you have laws about what you could wear, yellow stars--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that come up in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: It did. It did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: It came up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a year later. We had to wear the yellow star. And then it came the curfew, with the yellow star. And we got protected. Not to leave the house. Just to be around our own people. And we were scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scared. At what point were you told that you had to leave your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944 on Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: This would be a good time to change our tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
